sentences.text: Charles Karl Hirsch.
H-I-R-S-C-H.
No, no other names.
September 19th, 1914.
I'll be 81 in September.
I was born in Vienna, which is now Austria.
At the time of my birth, it was the Austro-Hungarian Kingdom-- monarchy.
V-- as in victory-- I-E-N-NA.
A-U-S-T-R-I-A.
Well, so not to go to extremes, I'm almost tempted to say marvelous.
It was a very cosmopolitan, intellectual city-- the wellspring of many movements in the arts, in medicine, and not to speak of, psychology.
My father, who admired the old monarchy, ascribed to the fact that Austria lived in peace since 1848 under the emperor.
And that long period of peace permitted the growth and flourishing of the various arts, sciences, and commerce, as well.

In addition to the fact that the Jews during the '48 revolution were released from the ghetto, permitted them within a few years to gain tremendous prominence in the arts, in the literature, in the sciences, medicine, and particularly in commerce, in the old Austrian monarchy.
My recollections of Vienna before the war are most pleasant.
I really, as a youngster, did not encounter anti-Semitism, I must say, despite the fact that I moved in mixed groups in my school.
One of my best friends was a very devout Catholic, whom I will later mention.
Of course, nothing is permanent.
Nothing is fixed.
Nothing is stable.
Everything is in flux.
And it certainly changed rapidly.
I remember it very distinctly.
We lived in the 2nd district, which, before '48, was the Jewish ghetto, and maintained a very intensive Jewish population.
The saving grace was that we lived right at the border.
It was the 1st district, which was the finest, so it wasn't too bad.
But anyway, I lived in Hollandstrasse, Holland Street number 2.
Our apartment door was number 49.
It was right near a bridge going over the canal.
Vienna is thought of being on the Danube, but it really isn't.
The Danube is quite far away from the center of town.
Vienna actually straddles the Danube Canal.
No, I mentioned '49.
That's what I mentioned before.
During the revolution, when the young emperor got onto the throne, the Jewish ghetto was opened.
Jews were permitted then to move to other sections of the city.

And as I said before, there was a tremendous flow of adrenalin, I presume, which made the Jews really [? climb up. ?] I will put in this caveat, however-- in order to become a university professor, a Jew had to have himself baptized.
So there were certain restrictions.
However, there were plenty Jewish university professors.
Well, let's say of Jewish extraction.
No.
In that district, the 1st-- the 1st district was the center, the original nucleus of Vienna.
And all surrounding districts were apartment houses.
Further out, toward the outskirts, were private homes.
So this was an apartment house of mixed occupancy.
I will say that downstairs was a big courtyard.
And in one of the corners, a shul opened later.
I will come back to that.
And that becomes very important later on in my story.
My father's name was Hugo--H-U-G-O-- David Hirsch.
Both my father and mother were born in what was then Bohemia.
Today it would be-- not anymore-- Czechoslovakia.
Today, it would be the Czech Republic since the breakup of the two countries.

And my father was born in the northern section, which was called Sudetenland, which is the border between Bohemia and Germany, and the area that Hitler invaded first because he had many sympathizers there, because that was a border region that spoke predominantly German.
My father did not speak Czech, except for a few dirty words that he learned in the army.

My mother, in turn, was born in the heart of Bohemia, in the very center, where Czech nationalism was very strong-- the area of Jan Hus, the reformer who was actually burned at the stake, an early Protestant forerunner in central Europe.
And my mother, in turn, was very Czech-oriented, spoke Czech fluently, taught us three children to speak Czech-- which I still do very well-- and an interesting-- well, let's go back to my father.
My father came from a family that had a clothing store.
And his father died when he was very young.
And his mother, together with two brothers and a sister, continued the store and supported them.
And my father was a mama's boy for the rest of his life.
Very much attached to her.
He was extremely musical.
Taught himself to play the zither.
But when his mother died, he put the zither on top of a closet and said, this is it.
And he never touched it again.
That's an insight into his sensitivity.
My mother, in turn, came from a very large family.
Her father was dealing in cattle.
And they had 13 children.
Most of them flourished.
And without--
--becoming facetious, it was the thing in those days to have a lot of children, whatever the mentality was.
Well, I have given it to you.
Can you help me to refresh my memory?
It is July 18th, 1874.
Yes.
My mother's name was Wilhelmina, maiden name Sabbath.
S-A-B-B-A-T-H.
OK.
And, of course, her birth was July 1st, 1885.
Yes.
Well, like it was done in those days.
The aunt of my mother was living in Vienna and married to my father's brother.
My mother came to visit her aunt, my father came to visit his brother, and they were introduced.
And that's the way it was done in those days.
And they married.
They--
Well, since I was born-- my brother was born in 1911.
It was before that.
That's how it was done in those days.
[LAUGHS]
My brother, Walter, four years older.
My sister, Herta, who changed her name to Harriet, four years younger.
We had a wonderful home life, really.
My father was a wonderful joke-teller, and I presume all three of us picked that up from him.
And we really had a very close home life, I must say.
Also conditioned by the fact that every summer, our parents would send us off to Bohemia to my grandfather's farm.
And there we met numerous cousins and aunts and uncles who came there.
It was like a common meeting ground for the family.
And that, of course, made for a very closely woven, broader family than just our own family.
And many of my fondest memories go back to those times at my grandfather's farm.
He was a wonderful man, in my opinion.
I'm trying to do the same.
He used to take me by the hand and take me through the fields and show me the corn and the potatoes that he was growing.
Various other experiences that I had with him.
It was really pleasant.
And he was able to dominate this entire crowd of little children by simulating saying, I'm going to take my belt.
And as soon as he put his hand over here, we scurried about, and that was it.
So we all behaved reasonably well.
We did have, as I think back, a nice family life.
We had, by today's standards, a large apartment, although only one bathroom.
Buildings in Vienna were old.
Some of them were 100, 200 years old.
And they're still standing today.
And so we were middle class, reasonably comfortable.
My father and his brother, the one I mentioned before, had a business manufacturing ladies' clothings and garments.
And it flourished very much in the beginning.
Later on, it tapered off because they didn't go with the times.
They didn't mechanize.
Happens.
I and my brother, we shared a room.
Yes, we did.
It was commonplace in Vienna to have sleep-in maids came-- coming from the provinces.
It was commonplace.
No.
No, they were not.
Did you have tutors at your home?
Did you-- did you receive musical--
Yes, my mother was very much into education, and we had a French tutor at home.
And we had a violin teacher come in to teach my brother to play the violin, but he was usually not around for the lesson.
So I had to pitch in.
And really, neither one of us learned much from this sporadic here and there.
Well, we spoke German, of course.
But with my mother, we spoke Czech many a times.
Yes.
My father was the sales part of the business.
He traveled most of the year.
My uncle was the inside man.
The office was right in the business, and the business was in the same building, downstairs.
Yes.
That is the crux of it.
They gave out work to home workers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They would get all the material and deliver the finished goods.
When things changed later, and people did garment manufacturing in-house, they failed to adjust to that.
It was a slow process.
But it's-- But it's quite-- well, 'confectionery' here has a different connotation, but in German, it means making dresses-- manufacturing them.
So there was a changeover in technique, which they didn't follow swiftly enough.
No.
That was difficult in those days.
Remember, there were no cars.
He had to arrange a trip in advance for months, schedule these huge bags in which he had all the samples of the, what was called, 'collection.' And that had to be scheduled way in advance, and no.
He did send us postcards from everywhere, including apples from northern Italy.
But that was about as much as we got out of it.
Well, I was an introvert-- very much so.
I was very studious.
I was-- I graduated whatever school I was in with the highest honors, including the technical university.
I want to bring this up now.
I graduated with the highest honors.
There were only four Jews in my engineering class, and I was the only one to get this honor.
And I want to stress this-- that in the field of sciences, when somebody was good, the fact that I was Jewish didn't seem to influence the professors.
In my-- in my papers at the school, it says specifically [GERMAN].
It means 'citizenship, Austria.' [GERMAN], which means 'people association-- Jewish.' And every time I went to an exam, I had to present this book.
It was not a question of passing or denying, no matter how [INAUDIBLE] you wore lederhosen or not, which was a conventional dress in Austria.
They knew I was Jewish.
I can't say that I felt, until the time of Hitler, that I felt this problem.
I'll be honest about it.
I will say that as soon as students became radicalized, they started to beat us up.
And this very friend whom I want to mention-- Ludwig [?
Prater ?] was his name-- his brother was a strong Nazi.
And Ludwig used to warn me every time, stay home today, Karl.
They're going to beat you guys up.
And I would.
I want to bring these things out to show the ambivalence in life.
Well, that was in '37.
Just prior.
Yes.
Prior.
Well, because engineering was not considered the Jewish trade in those days.
Well, the tradition was toward more different learnings-- medicine, law, accounting, as I mentioned before.
And engineers, really, it was also difficult to get a job for an engineer in Austria-- for a Jewish engineer, I will admit.
And it was always my plan to go to Austria anyway.
But once I graduated, things changed.
Well, there was anti-Semitism throughout industry.
The engineering field is, or at least was-- also in this country, as I will later relate-- a Christian field.

And it's only with the advent of electronics where the analytical mind, which is typical of Jews and also of certain Asians, came to the fore, rather than the space technology type of mind that is in machine construction, et cetera, that was needed before.
When that mind became more in demand, the field opened up for Jews.
There was no more question about it--
--in this country.
I don't-- you may agree or you may not agree, but that's my view of it.
No, no.
This was just to tell you why so few students were Jewish at the technical university.
Well, you know, [INAUDIBLE] whether we liked it or not, a Jewish education was obligatory for the following reason.
Austria, just like Poland, had a, what was called, concordat, which means an agreement with the pope or with the Catholic church.
It was a Catholic state.
Catholicism was the state religion.
However, beginning with grade school, through what we'd call gymnasium, which would be upper school here, one hour a week was set aside for religious instruction.
And it was obligatory to attend it.
You were graded.
Those pupils that didn't keep up-- we had to learn Hebrew conjugation of verbs, which I remember to this day.
It doesn't help me much, but I can still go through it.
If you failed, you wouldn't pass that year.
So it was really an obligatory session.
You-- the thing that-- that-- up to the year when you are 5 or 6, you really don't know the difference.
Then you go to school, and at a certain time of the day, they go there and we go here.
And that started to develop a certain distance.
Now, each classroom, of course, had Christ on the cross.
We were facing it when we were sitting in our chairs.
And so the aura that permeated the school was Catholic.
There was a monsignori assigned to each school.
With his purple robes, he used to shuffle through the hallways, and we had to greet him properly.
That is the way it was.
I don't know how it is today.
I haven't had any contact with young people in Austria lately.
But that is how it was.

And one is amazed that, despite the fact that Austria was 98% Catholic-- the number of Jews among 6 million was 200,000, a small percentage and very few Protestants-- that they went so overboard for Hitler.
It's an interesting phenomenon because it's really the antithesis of Catholicism.
Hitler was really bound to reinstitute the old German gods, Wotan and Freyr, Loki, et cetera.
That was really his thrust.
Yes.
Well, it was located in the Seitenstettenstrasse.
It was called the Seitenstetten Temple.
It was in the 1st district.
It was probably the number one synagogue in Vienna.
It was a beautiful building.
You go in there now, it's a magnificent building.
A huge rotunda with columns.
That was back there, some years back.
Seitenstetten.
S-E-I-T-E-N-S-T-E-double T-E-N.
It's the same as the street that it was on.
And my brother and I both were bar mitzvahed there.

It was a tradition, regardless of whether the family was very religious or not, because it was a-- an impetus for the relatives-- in our case, from Czechoslovakia-- to come down there for that occasion and see everybody and bring the obligatory Omega watch, which I still have.
And the chief rabbi in Vienna at that time was Oberrabbiner-- top rabbi-- Chayes--
--who was really well-known in central Europe.
He was esteemed as a savant.
C-H-A-Y-E-S.
Well, I really cannot answer that question, but I don't think there was a father by the name of Chayes before him.
I didn't hear that.
So I presume there, it was more by knowledge and learning and the esteem that he was able to garner as he climbed up in the hierarchy.
I remember it fondly because I was really short and I had to stand on a rather high pedestal to read the scripture.
I don't recall the chapter.
It's probably in-- in the [? memento ?] that I have.
And I'd like to say it was a family occasion.
That's what it was.
Same for my brother.
There were no bat mitzvahs in those days.
That is a new procedure.
And so my sister was not involved in that.
My father-- there's a little thing I want to tell you which has stuck with me the rest of my life, even though I don't do it.
My father used to get us together on Friday and bless us.
And even though his Hebrew was very limited, he made it through the [HEBREW].
And that has stuck with me all my life.
And while I haven't visited a synagogue except on family occasions-- bar mitzvahs or weddings-- I do listen regularly to WQXR.
And when it comes to the blessings, I really-- it rips open all this memory, and it really means something to me.
And that is the tradition that stays with me.
I haven't continued that with my children for other reasons.
But when you're born a Jew, you're a Jew.
Doesn't matter whether you are religious, whether you believe in every little-- there is no dot on the i, but whether you believe in every i, et cetera.
I believe it's something that's evolved over thousands of years.
I'm not a biologist.
I don't know everything in your genes.
But I'm almost tempted to say it is.
And the events that you go through sometimes minimize it and sometimes accentuate it, but the level stays there.
And whether your feeling is strong or whether your feeling is not strong, it is there.
I will-- at my ripe age of denial, I'll agree to that.
I don't-- we didn't have that type of framework.
I think he was recognized as the top rabbi in Vienna, which was the center of this 60 million-people empire.
And so he had quite a standing.
But as far as being, if I can use the term, a cult leader or something like that, no such thing.
No way.
It wasn't.
My mother did have one of the helpers in the business bring up this huge box with all these dishes to have different dishes on Passover.
But there's an interesting side [? to it. ?] My mother would eat ham.
She would first put a piece of paper on the dish, and then have a ham sandwich.
So I want to show you how-- how different different practices are used in different surroundings and in different phases of Jewish culture.
Well, Passover, on account of the food that was served.
My mother was a wonderful cook.
And that, of course, stands out in a young lad's mind.
To this day, I wouldn't eat on Yom Kippur.
I don't keep anything else.
But I wouldn't eat on Yom Kippur regardless, period.
I consider it an exercise of tradition, self-control, and that's it.
Fine.
